name: Passerellidae
description: Passerellidae, the New World sparrows, are a large and diverse family of passerine birds found primarily in the Americas. They are seed-eating birds with conical bills, often with streaked brown plumage and melodious songs.

children.name: Zonotrichia
children.description: A genus of large, handsome sparrows with distinctive crown patterns, found across North America.

children.name: Zonotrichia leucophrys
children.description: The White-crowned Sparrow is a large sparrow with bold black-and-white crown stripes and a pink bill. It breeds across northern North America and winters throughout much of the United States.

children.name: Zonotrichia albicollis
children.description: The White-throated Sparrow has a distinctive white throat, yellow lores, and black-and-white crown stripes. It breeds in Canadian forests and winters across the eastern United States.

children.name: Zonotrichia atricapilla
children.description: A large sparrow with a bright yellow crown patch bordered by black, grey breast, and brown upperparts. It breeds in the shrubby tundra of Alaska and western Canada, wintering along the Pacific coast of the United States.

children.name: Zonotrichia querula
children.description: A large, chunky sparrow with a black face and bib, pale grey breast, and brown upperparts with heavy streaking. It breeds in the northern boreal forest of Canada and winters in the Great Plains of the United States.

children.name: Zonotrichia spathz
children.description: This newly described species, Zonotrichia spathz, is a small passerine bird known for its distinct song and preference for forested habitats.

children.name: Melospiza
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, heavily streaked sparrows found across North America.

children.name: Melospiza melodia
children.description: The Song Sparrow is a brown, heavily streaked sparrow with a central breast spot and a rich, melodious song. It is one of the most widespread and variable sparrows in North America.

children.name: Melospiza lincolnii
children.description: A handsome sparrow with a finely streaked buffy breast, a grey face, and a distinctive white eye-ring. It breeds across northern North America and in western mountain ranges, wintering in the southern United States and Central America.

children.name: Melospiza georgiana
children.description: A small sparrow of marshes and wetlands with a rich rufous crown, grey breast, and dark streaked back. It breeds across northern North America and winters in the southern and eastern United States.

children.name: Centronyx
children.description: A small genus of grassland sparrows formerly placed in Ammodramus, found in the prairies of North America.

children.name: Centronyx bairdii
children.description: A secretive sparrow of the northern Great Plains with a buffy breast with fine streaking, a yellow supraloral stripe, and a short notched tail. It breeds in native mixed-grass prairies of Canada and the northern United States, wintering in the southwestern states and northern Mexico.

children.name: Centronyx henslowii
children.description: A small, secretive sparrow with a flat head, olive-green face, heavily streaked back, and a short tail. It inhabits dense grasslands and prairies of the eastern and central United States, with a distinctive short, insect-like song.

children.name: Spizella
children.description: A genus of small, slender sparrows with rufous crowns and plain underparts, found across North America.

children.name: Spizella passerina
children.description: The Chipping Sparrow is a small, neat sparrow with a bright rufous crown, grey breast, and white supercilium. It is common in open woodlands and suburban areas across most of North America.

children.name: Spizella pallida
children.description: A pale, slender sparrow with a buffy face with dark cheek patch, a pale median crown stripe, and a whitish breast. It breeds in brushy grasslands of the northern Great Plains and winters in Texas and Mexico.

children.name: Spizella breweri
children.description: A small, plain grey-brown sparrow with fine dark streaking on the back and a long, buzzy trilled song. It breeds in sagebrush steppes of the western United States and Canada, wintering in the southwestern states and Mexico.

children.name: Spizella pusilla
children.description: A small, delicate sparrow with a pink bill, rufous crown, white eye-ring, and plain grey breast. It inhabits old fields and scrubby grasslands of eastern and central North America, singing a series of clear, accelerating whistles.

children.name: Spizella atrogularis
children.description: A distinctive sparrow with a grey body, brown streaked back, and a black chin and throat patch in breeding adults. It inhabits arid scrub and chaparral of the southwestern United States and Mexico.

children.name: Spizella wortheni
children.description: A pale sparrow with a grey breast, rufous crown, and a distinctive white eye-ring, similar to the Field Sparrow. It is endemic to the highlands of northeastern Mexico and is considered endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Junco
children.description: A genus of sparrows with grey or brown plumage and white outer tail feathers, found across North America.

children.name: Junco hyemalis
children.description: The Dark-eyed Junco is a variable sparrow with dark grey or brown plumage, pink bill, and white outer tail feathers. It is a common winter visitor across most of the United States.

children.name: Junco phaeonotus
children.description: A junco with a grey head and breast, brown back, bright yellow eyes, and pink bill with dark tip. It is found in montane pine-oak forests of Mexico and the southwestern United States.

children.name: Junco vulcani
children.description: A dark junco with a grey head, brown back, and pale pinkish bill, endemic to the high volcanic peaks of Costa Rica and western Panama. It inhabits paramo grasslands and montane scrub above 2500 metres.

children.name: Junco insularis
children.description: A distinctive junco endemic to Guadalupe Island off Baja California, with dark grey upperparts, a black face, and a white belly. It is considered endangered due to feral cat predation and habitat degradation.

children.name: Passerella
children.description: A genus of large, heavily built sparrows with variable rufous and grey plumage.

children.name: Passerella iliaca
children.description: The Fox Sparrow is a large, plump sparrow with heavy reddish-brown streaking and a spotted breast. It is a skulking bird that breeds in northern forests and winters across the southern United States.

children.name: Passerella unalaschcensis
children.description: A dark, sooty-brown fox sparrow with heavy streaking on the breast and a greyish face. It breeds along the Pacific coast from Alaska to British Columbia, inhabiting coastal rainforests and thickets.

children.name: Passerella schistacea
children.description: A pale greyish fox sparrow with a grey head and back, white underparts with dark spotting, and a rufous tail. It breeds in the interior mountains of western North America, inhabiting dry coniferous forests.

children.name: Passerella megarhyncha
children.description: A large fox sparrow with a notably thick, heavy bill, grey head, and rufous wings and tail. It breeds in the Sierra Nevada and Cascade ranges, inhabiting montane chaparral and forest edges.

children.name: Peucaea
children.description: A genus of medium-sized sparrows with stout bills and often rufous wings, found in arid and semi-arid habitats from the southern United States through Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Peucaea botterii
children.description: A pale, slender sparrow with a long tail, streaked brown back, and a plain breast with a central spot. It inhabits grasslands and savannas from the southwestern United States to Costa Rica.

children.name: Peucaea cassinii
children.description: A pale, secretive sparrow of dry grasslands with a streaked back, white underparts, and a unique skylarking song flight. It breeds in the southern Great Plains of the United States and northern Mexico.

children.name: Peucaea aestivalis
children.description: A large, plain sparrow with a rufous crown, grey face, and unstreaked buffy breast. It inhabits pine forests and clearings in the southeastern United States, singing a beautiful, rich song from exposed perches.

children.name: Peucaea carpalis
children.description: A small sparrow with a grey face, rufous crown and wings, and a black whisker stripe. It is endemic to the Sonoran Desert region of Arizona and Mexico, inhabiting thorn scrub and grassy areas.

children.name: Peucaea ruficauda
children.description: A striking sparrow with a black-and-white striped head, rufous wings and tail, and a white breast with a dark central spot. It ranges from Mexico to Costa Rica in dry scrub and woodland edges.

children.name: Peucaea humeralis
children.description: A distinctive sparrow with a black breast and throat, white face, grey crown, and rufous wings. It is found in arid scrub and thorn forests from Mexico to Costa Rica.

children.name: Artemisiospiza
children.description: A genus of pale, desert-dwelling sparrows formerly placed in Amphispiza, found in sagebrush and saltbush habitats of western North America.

children.name: Artemisiospiza belli
children.description: A pale sparrow with a dark breast spot, white outer tail feathers, and a long tail. It inhabits coastal sage scrub and saltbush flats of California and Baja California.

children.name: Artemisiospiza nevadensis
children.description: A pale sparrow with a dark breast spot, grey face, and white outer tail feathers. It breeds in sagebrush steppes of the interior western United States, wintering in the southwestern states and Mexico.

children.name: Pooecetes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Vesper Sparrow, a long-bodied sparrow of open grasslands and fields across North America.

children.name: Pooecetes gramineus
children.description: A large, pale sparrow of open grasslands with a white eye-ring, white outer tail feathers, and a small chestnut patch on the shoulder. It sings a beautiful, melodic song reminiscent of a Song Sparrow but sweeter, often delivered from a fence post or wire.

children.name: Passerculus
children.description: A widespread genus of small, streaked sparrows found in grasslands across North America. The Savannah Sparrow complex shows remarkable variation across subspecies, some of which were once considered separate species.

children.name: Passerculus sandwichensis
children.description: A small, streaked sparrow with a yellowish stripe above the eye, a short notched tail, and pinkish legs. It breeds across grasslands and tundra from Alaska and Canada through the western United States, wintering in the southern half of the continent.

children.name: Ammodramus
children.description: A genus of chunky, short-tailed sparrows adapted to dense grasslands, found across North America. They are notoriously secretive and are more often heard than seen, but their insect-like songs carry far across open fields.

children.name: Ammodramus savannarum
children.description: A small, flat-headed sparrow with a short tail, a pale central crown stripe, and a distinctive buzzy, insect-like song that resembles a grasshopper. It inhabits dry grasslands and prairies across much of North America, wintering in the southern United States and Mexico.

children.name: Ammodramus humeralis
children.description: A small, streaked sparrow with a yellowish breast and a short tail, found in grasslands of South America from Colombia to Argentina. It inhabits open savannas and pastures.

children.name: Ammospiza
children.description: A genus of sparrows specialised for life in coastal salt marshes, tidal creeks, and brackish wetlands along the coasts of North America. They have distinctive, rather flat heads and stout bills adapted for a diet of small crustaceans and insects in addition to seeds.

children.name: Ammospiza nelsoni
children.description: A small, furtive sparrow of salt marshes with a buff-orange face and breast, grey nape, and heavily streaked back. It breeds in freshwater and brackish marshes of interior Canada and along the Atlantic coast, wintering in coastal marshes of the southeastern United States.

children.name: Ammospiza maritima
children.description: A pale greyish sparrow with a yellow patch before the eye, a long bill, and a short tail, confined to salt marshes along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the United States. It is vulnerable to habitat loss and sea-level rise, with the extinct Dusky Seaside Sparrow as a subspecies.

children.name: Ammospiza caudacuta
children.description: A sharply dressed sparrow of tidal marshes with a bright orange face, grey nape, and heavily streaked black-and-white back. It breeds in salt marshes along the northeastern coast of the United States and is highly vulnerable to sea-level rise.

children.name: Ammospiza leconteii
children.description: A tiny, secretive sparrow with a buff-orange face, grey nape, finely streaked back, and a short tail. It breeds in wet meadows and marshes of central Canada and the northern Great Plains, wintering in the southeastern United States.

children.name: Pipilo
children.description: A genus of large, handsome sparrows known as towhees, characterised by long tails, stout bills, and striking plumage patterns. They are primarily ground-foraging birds that scratch loudly through leaf litter with both feet in a backwards hop.

children.name: Pipilo chlorurus
children.description: A distinctive towhee with an olive-green back, a rufous crown, a white throat, and a grey face and breast. It breeds in mountain shrublands of the western United States, wintering in the southwestern states and Mexico.

children.name: Pipilo maculatus
children.description: A striking towhee with a black hood and upperparts spotted with white, rufous flanks, and a white belly. It is common in brushy habitats across western North America, scratching noisily through leaf litter.

children.name: Pipilo erythrophthalmus
children.description: A large, boldly patterned towhee of eastern North America with a black hood and upperparts (male), rufous flanks, and a white belly. Its distinctive call sounds like 'drink your tea!' and it is a vigorous leaf-scratcher in forest undergrowth.

children.name: Pipilo ocai
children.description: A large towhee with a black hood, white collar, rufous flanks, and a long tail. It is endemic to the highlands of Mexico, inhabiting pine-oak forests and cloud forests at middle to high elevations.

children.name: Melozone
children.description: A genus of medium-sized sparrows with often plain brown or strikingly patterned plumage, found from the southwestern United States through Mexico to Central America.

children.name: Melozone crissalis
children.description: A large, plain brown towhee with a long tail, a buffy throat and vent, and a notably dull appearance. It is endemic to California and Baja California, where it is a common and conspicuous resident of chaparral, gardens, and parks.

children.name: Melozone fusca
children.description: A pale grey-brown towhee with a rusty crown and vent, a central breast spot, and a long tail. It is found in arid canyons and scrublands from the southwestern United States through Mexico.

children.name: Melozone aberti
children.description: A plain brown towhee with a black face mask, pale bill, and a long tail. It is endemic to the Sonoran Desert of the southwestern United States and Mexico, closely associated with riparian corridors and mesquite thickets.

children.name: Melozone biarcuata
children.description: A striking ground sparrow with a white face bordered by black, a grey breast, and olive-brown upperparts. It is found from Mexico to Costa Rica, inhabiting forest edges and thickets.

children.name: Melozone leucotis
children.description: A handsome ground sparrow with a white ear patch, black crown and face stripes, olive upperparts, and a yellow belly. It ranges from Mexico to Nicaragua in humid forest undergrowth.

children.name: Melozone kieneri
children.description: A ground sparrow with a rusty crown, grey face, white throat, and olive-brown upperparts. It is endemic to western Mexico, inhabiting dry forests and scrub.

children.name: Aimophila
children.description: A genus of medium-sized sparrows with long tails and rusty crowns, found in arid and semi-arid scrublands from the southwestern United States through Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Aimophila ruficeps
children.description: A neat, long-tailed sparrow with a bright rufous crown, a grey face and breast, and a white throat. It inhabits rocky hillsides and dry scrub from California and Texas through Mexico.

children.name: Aimophila notosticta
children.description: A medium-sized sparrow with a rufous crown, grey face, heavily streaked back, and a long tail. It is endemic to the arid highlands of Oaxaca in southern Mexico, inhabiting dry scrub and rocky hillsides.

children.name: Amphispiza
children.description: A small genus of pale, desert-dwelling sparrows with striking facial patterns. They are well adapted to arid environments and are characteristic birds of the deserts and dry scrublands of western North America.

children.name: Amphispiza bilineata
children.description: A handsome sparrow of arid scrublands with a distinctive black bib, white supercilium, and plain grey-brown upperparts. It is a characteristic bird of the Mojave, Sonoran, and Chihuahuan deserts, where it sings a simple, tinkling song from exposed perches.

children.name: Chondestes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Lark Sparrow, one of the most distinctive North American sparrows with a striking facial pattern of chestnut, black, and white. It has a long, rounded tail and a stout seed-cracking bill.

children.name: Chondestes grammacus
children.description: A large, striking sparrow with a chestnut, black, and white facial pattern, a white belly with a central dark spot, and white corners on the tail. It breeds in open grasslands and savannas across central and western North America, wintering in Mexico and the southern United States.

children.name: Rhynchospiza
children.description: A small genus of sparrows found in arid and semi-arid scrublands of western South America. They were formerly included in Aimophila and have stout, conical bills adapted for seed-cracking.

children.name: Rhynchospiza stolzmanni
children.description: A medium-sized sparrow with a streaked brown back, grey breast, and a distinctive facial pattern with a white supercilium and dark cheek patch. It is found in arid scrub of the Tumbesian region of Ecuador and Peru.

children.name: Rhynchospiza strigiceps
children.description: A sparrow with a rufous crown with a pale median stripe, grey face, and streaked brown upperparts. It inhabits dry scrub and grasslands of southern South America from Bolivia to Argentina.

children.name: Pezopetes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Large-footed Finch, a large, terrestrial sparrow of montane forests in Central America. It is named for its unusually large, powerful feet adapted for scratching through leaf litter.

children.name: Pezopetes capitalis
children.description: A large, olive-brown sparrow with a grey face, bright yellow throat and breast, and notably large feet. It is endemic to the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama, inhabiting dense undergrowth in montane forests above 2000 metres.

children.name: Torreornis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Zapata Sparrow, a secretive, ground-dwelling sparrow endemic to Cuba and one of the most restricted-range passerines in the Americas.

children.name: Torreornis inexpectata
children.description: A medium-sized sparrow with olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, and a grey head with a white supercilium. It is endemic to Cuba, with three isolated subspecies occupying different habitats: Zapata Swamp, the Isle of Youth, and montane scrub in eastern Cuba.

children.name: Xenospiza
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Sierra Madre Sparrow, a highly range-restricted species endemic to highland grasslands of Mexico. It is threatened by habitat loss and agricultural intensification.

children.name: Xenospiza baileyi
children.description: A small sparrow with a rufous crown, grey face, heavily streaked brown back, and a short tail. It is endemic to high-altitude bunchgrass meadows of the Sierra Madre Occidental in Mexico, where it is threatened by habitat conversion to agriculture.

children.name: Arremonops
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, olive-backed sparrows with striped heads, found from southern Texas through Mexico and Central America to northern South America.

children.name: Arremonops rufivirgatus
children.description: A plain olive-brown sparrow with a striped head pattern of brown and buff, a buffy breast, and a long tail. It inhabits dense undergrowth and thickets from southern Texas through Mexico to Costa Rica.

children.name: Arremonops chloronotus
children.description: A sparrow with bright olive-green upperparts, a grey head with black-and-white stripes, and pale underparts. It is found in humid lowland forests from southern Mexico to Honduras.

children.name: Arremonops conirostris
children.description: A large sparrow with olive-green upperparts, a grey head with bold black stripes, and a yellow wash on the belly. It ranges from Honduras through Central America to northern South America in humid lowland forests.

children.name: Arremon
children.description: A genus of sparrows and brush finches with striking black, white, and olive plumage patterns, found from Mexico through Central and South America. They inhabit forest undergrowth and are often more easily heard than seen.

children.name: Arremon aurantiirostris
children.description: A striking sparrow with a bright orange bill, black-and-white striped head, olive-green upperparts, and yellow belly. It ranges from southern Mexico through Central America to northern South America in humid lowland forests.

children.name: Arremon assimilis
children.description: A brush finch with a grey head with white stripes, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It is found in the Andes from Venezuela to Peru, inhabiting montane forest undergrowth.

children.name: Arremon brunneinucha
children.description: A large brush finch with a chestnut crown, black face with white throat, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It ranges from Mexico to Peru in montane forests, often in dense undergrowth near streams.

children.name: Arremon crassirostris
children.description: A distinctive finch with a sooty-black face, olive-green upperparts, white throat, and yellow belly. It is endemic to the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama, inhabiting dense undergrowth in cloud forests.

children.name: Arremon flavirostris
children.description: A sparrow with a bright yellow bill, black-and-white striped head, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It is found in lowland forests and scrub of southern South America from Brazil to Argentina.

children.name: Arremon semitorquatus
children.description: A sparrow with a black breast band across the white underparts, olive-green upperparts, and a black-and-white striped head. It is endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil.

children.name: Arremon franciscanus
children.description: A recently described sparrow with a black-and-white striped head, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts with a narrow black breast band. It is endemic to dry forests of the Sao Francisco River valley in Brazil.

children.name: Arremon castaneiceps
children.description: A small finch with a chestnut crown, olive-green upperparts, pale grey underparts, and a short tail. It is found in the Andes from Colombia to Peru, inhabiting montane forest undergrowth.

children.name: Arremon laniirostris
children.description: A dull olive-brown brush finch with a grey head, pale throat, and a stout black bill. It ranges from Costa Rica through Central America to western Ecuador in humid lowland and premontane forests.

children.name: Arremon schlegeli
children.description: A striking sparrow with a black-and-white striped head, golden-yellow wing patch, olive upperparts, and white underparts. It inhabits dry scrub and forests of northern Colombia and Venezuela.

children.name: Atlapetes
children.description: A large genus of colorful brush finches with rufous, yellow, and olive plumage, found in montane forests and scrublands of Central and South America. They are active, often conspicuous birds that forage in pairs or small groups.

children.name: Atlapetes latinuchus
children.description: A bright brush finch with a black head, yellow breast and belly, olive-green upperparts, and a yellow shoulder patch. It is found in the Andes from Colombia to Ecuador, inhabiting montane scrub and forest edges.

children.name: Atlapetes leucopterus
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, white wing patches, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It is found in the Andes of Peru and Ecuador, inhabiting montane scrub and cloud forest edges.

children.name: Atlapetes pallidinucha
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, a distinctive pale patch on the nape, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. It ranges from Colombia to Peru in high-altitude scrub and forest edges.

children.name: Atlapetes semirufus
children.description: A colorful brush finch with a black head, rufous breast and belly, olive-green upperparts, and white throat patches. It is found in the Andes of Colombia and Venezuela in montane forests and scrub.

children.name: Atlapetes melanocephalus
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, bright yellow underparts, olive-green upperparts, and a yellow shoulder patch. It is endemic to the Santa Marta Mountains of Colombia, inhabiting montane scrub and forest edges.

children.name: Atlapetes pileatus
children.description: A brush finch with a rufous crown, grey face, olive-green back, and pale underparts. It is endemic to the highlands of Mexico, inhabiting oak and pine-oak forests.

children.name: Atlapetes albinucha
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, a white patch on the nape, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It ranges from Colombia to Peru in montane forests and scrub.

children.name: Atlapetes gutturalis
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, bright yellow throat, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It is found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru.

children.name: Atlapetes personatus
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, yellow underparts, olive-green upperparts, and a yellow shoulder patch. It is found on the tepuis of southern Venezuela, Guyana, and northern Brazil.

children.name: Atlapetes seebohmi
children.description: A brush finch with a rufous crown, grey face, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. It is endemic to the Andes of Peru, inhabiting montane scrub and Polylepis woodlands.

children.name: Atlapetes nationi
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, rufous belly, olive-green upperparts, and white throat. It is endemic to the arid Andes of southern Peru, inhabiting dry scrub and cactus groves.

children.name: Atlapetes canigenis
children.description: A brush finch with a black crown, grey ear patches, olive-green upperparts, and yellowish underparts. It is endemic to the Andes of central Peru, inhabiting cloud forest undergrowth.

children.name: Atlapetes schistaceus
children.description: A dark brush finch with a slaty-grey head and breast, olive-green upperparts, and white belly. It is found in the Andes from Colombia to Peru.

children.name: Atlapetes rufigenis
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, rufous ear patches, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is endemic to the highlands of central Peru.

children.name: Atlapetes citrinellus
children.description: A brush finch with a black-and-yellow striped head, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is endemic to northwestern Argentina, inhabiting montane forests and scrub.

children.name: Atlapetes leucopis
children.description: A brush finch with a black head, distinctive white eye-ring and face patches, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is found in the Andes of Ecuador and Colombia.

children.name: Atlapetes tricolor
children.description: A colorful brush finch with a black head, rufous breast band, yellow belly, and olive-green upperparts. It is endemic to the Andes of Peru.

children.name: Atlapetes flaviceps
children.description: A brush finch with a bright yellow head and throat, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is endemic to the Andes of Colombia and is considered vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Atlapetes fuscoolivaceus
children.description: A brush finch with a dark olive-grey head, olive-green upperparts, and yellowish underparts. It is endemic to the Andes of Colombia.

children.name: Atlapetes nigrifrons
children.description: A brush finch with a black forehead and face, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is found in the Santa Marta Mountains of Colombia.

children.name: Chlorospingus
children.description: A genus of small, olive-and-yellow birds formerly placed in the tanager family but now considered New World sparrows. They are active, social birds of montane forests from Mexico to South America.

children.name: Chlorospingus flavopectus
children.description: A small, olive-green bird with a yellow breast and throat, a white postocular spot, and a dark bill. It is the most widespread bush tanager, ranging from Mexico to Bolivia in montane forests.

children.name: Chlorospingus semifuscus
children.description: A dark bush tanager with greyish-olive upperparts, pale grey underparts, and a white throat. It is found in the Andes of Colombia and Ecuador.

children.name: Chlorospingus flavovirens
children.description: A bright bush tanager with olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, and a yellowish supercilium. It is endemic to the lowland forests of western Ecuador.

children.name: Chlorospingus canigularis
children.description: A bush tanager with olive-green upperparts, grey throat and breast, yellow belly, and a white postocular spot. It is found from Costa Rica to Peru in montane forests.

children.name: Chlorospingus parvirostris
children.description: A small bush tanager with olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, a short stout bill, and a white spot behind the eye. It is found in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia.

children.name: Chlorospingus pileatus
children.description: A bush tanager with a sooty-black crown, olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, and white postocular spot. It is endemic to the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama.

children.name: Chlorospingus flavigularis
children.description: A bush tanager with olive-green upperparts, a bright yellow throat, grey breast, and white belly. It is found in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia.

children.name: Chlorospingus tacarcunae
children.description: A bush tanager with olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, and a white postocular spot. It is endemic to the Tacarcuna region of Panama and Colombia.

children.name: Chlorospingus inornatus
children.description: A bush tanager with dull olive-green upperparts, pale grey underparts, and a white throat. It is endemic to Cerro Pirre in eastern Panama.

children.name: Chlorospingus novaeguineae
children.description: A bush tanager with olive-green upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a blackish crown. It is found in the Darien highlands of Panama and Colombia.

children.name: Oreothraupis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Tanager Finch, a distinctive bird with a thick bill and striking black-and-white head pattern. It was once considered a tanager but is now placed in the New World sparrow family.

children.name: Oreothraupis arremonops
children.description: A finch with a black-and-white striped head, olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, and a stout black bill. It is endemic to the cloud forests of the western Andes of Colombia and Ecuador.

children.name: Pipilopsis
children.description: A small genus of sparrows with boldly striped heads, found in the Andes of South America.

children.name: Pipilopsis leucoptera
children.description: A sparrow with a black-and-white striped head, olive-brown upperparts, and white underparts. It is found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Pipilopsis boliviana
children.description: A sparrow with a striped head, brown upperparts, and pale underparts. It is endemic to the highlands of Bolivia.

children.name: Haplospiza
children.description: A small genus of finches with uniformly dark grey or slaty plumage in males, found in Central and South America. They inhabit forest edges and clearings, feeding on grass seeds and bamboo seeds.

children.name: Haplospiza unicolor
children.description: A small finch with uniformly dark slaty-grey plumage in males, brownish in females, and a conical bill. It is found in forests of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina.

children.name: Haplospiza rustica
children.description: A finch with dark slaty-grey plumage in males, olive-brown in females, and a distinctive white stripe on the wing. It ranges from Mexico through Central America to the Andes of South America.

children.name: Acanthidops
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Peg-billed Finch, a distinctive bird of high-altitude scrub in Central America. It has a uniquely shaped, upturned bill adapted for extracting seeds from bamboo flowers.

children.name: Acanthidops barbatus
children.description: A small finch with a distinctive pale, upturned bill, grey upperparts, white underparts, and white outer tail feathers. It is found in highland scrub and bamboo thickets of Costa Rica and western Panama.

children.name: Spizellopsis
children.description: A genus of finches with streaked brown plumage, found in grasslands and open habitats of South America.

children.name: Spizellopsis brunneiventris
children.description: A finch with streaked brown upperparts, brownish underparts, and a pale supercilium. It is found in grasslands of southern South America.

children.name: Geospizopsis
children.description: A genus of plain-coloured finches found in the high Andes of South America, with grey or brown plumage and conical bills.

children.name: Geospizopsis unicolor
children.description: A finch with uniformly grey plumage, a conical orange-yellow bill, and white outer tail feathers. It is found in the high Andes from Colombia to Argentina.

children.name: Geospizopsis plumbea
children.description: A finch with lead-grey plumage, a black bill, and white outer tail feathers. It is found in the high Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Phrygilus
children.description: A genus of finches with grey, black, and white plumage, found in the high Andes of South America. They inhabit rocky slopes and dry scrub at high elevations.

children.name: Phrygilus gayi
children.description: A finch with a grey hood, yellow underparts, olive back, and white outer tail feathers. It is found in the Andes of Chile and Argentina.

children.name: Phrygilus punensis
children.description: A finch with a grey head and breast, yellow belly, and brownish back. It is found in the high Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Phrygilus atacamensis
children.description: A finch with a grey head, yellow underparts, and brown back. It is found in the arid highlands of Chile and Argentina.

children.name: Phrygilus patagonicus
children.description: A finch with a grey head, yellow underparts, olive back, and white outer tail feathers. It is found in Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego.

children.name: Phrygilus fruticeti
children.description: A finch with a black head and breast, grey back, and white belly in males. It is found in dry scrub of Argentina, Chile, and Bolivia.

children.name: Oryzoborus
children.description: A genus of finches with massive, thick bills adapted for cracking hard seeds, found in wet lowlands of Central and South America. They are sometimes placed in the tanager family but are now considered New World sparrows.

children.name: Oryzoborus crassirostris
children.description: A finch with a massive black bill, black body in males, and brown in females with a white wing patch. It is found in wet lowlands of northern South America.

children.name: Oryzoborus angolensis
children.description: A finch with a thick black bill, black head and upperparts, chestnut underparts in males. It ranges from Costa Rica through South America to Argentina.

children.name: Volatinia
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Blue-black Grassquit, a small finch of open and semi-open habitats in Central and South America. Males are glossy blue-black during the breeding season.

children.name: Volatinia jacarina
children.description: A tiny finch with glossy blue-black plumage in breeding males, brown in females, and a distinctive jumping display flight. It is found in grasslands and open areas from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Sporophila
children.description: A large genus of small, seed-eating finches with thick conical bills, found across Central and South America. Males often have striking black, white, and chestnut plumage patterns, while females are drab brown. Many species are popular cage birds.

children.name: Sporophila corvina
children.description: A small seedeater with variable black and white plumage, a thick conical bill, and a short tail. It ranges from Mexico through Central America to western Ecuador.

children.name: Sporophila americana
children.description: A seedeater with black upperparts, white underparts, and distinctive white wing bars. It is found in grasslands and open areas of northern South America.

children.name: Sporophila muralata
children.description: A small seedeater with black and white plumage, found in grasslands of southeastern Colombia.

children.name: Sporophila nigricollis
children.description: A seedeater with a black hood, yellow belly, olive back, and thick grey bill. It ranges from Costa Rica through South America to northern Argentina.

children.name: Sporophila luctuosa
children.description: A seedeater with black upperparts and breast, white belly and wing patches in males. It is found in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia.

children.name: Sporophila caerulescens
children.description: A seedeater with a black throat and breast band, white collar, yellow belly, and grey back in males. It is found in open habitats of southern South America.

children.name: Sporophila albogularis
children.description: A seedeater with a white throat, black breast band, chestnut belly, and grey back. It is endemic to northeastern Brazil.

children.name: Sporophila leucoptera
children.description: A seedeater with black upperparts, white underparts, and a massive pale bill. It is found in grasslands of central South America.

children.name: Sporophila bouvreuil
children.description: A small seedeater with rufous-chestnut underparts, grey back, and black wings. It is found in grasslands of central South America.

children.name: Sporophila minuta
children.description: A tiny seedeater with rufous underparts, grey back, and a small grey bill. It ranges from Mexico through Central America to northern South America.

children.name: Sporophila collaris
children.description: A seedeater with a black head, rufous collar, grey back, and white underparts. It is found in grasslands of central South America.

children.name: Sporophila falcirostris
children.description: A seedeater with a black head and breast, grey back, and white belly. It is found in the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Sporophila frontalis
children.description: A seedeater with a buff forehead, black crown, grey back, and white underparts. It is found in the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Sporophila nigra
children.description: A seedeater with completely black plumage in males, brown in females. It is endemic to the Antilles.

children.name: Sporophila albicauda
children.description: A seedeater with a black head, white tail, grey back, and white underparts. It is found in grasslands of central South America.

children.name: Sporophila palustris
children.description: A seedeater with a black head, white collar, rufous breast, grey back, and white belly. It is found in marshes of southern South America.

children.name: Sporophila cinerea
children.description: A seedeater with grey upperparts, white underparts, and a stout grey bill. It is found in grasslands of South America.

children.name: Sporophila melanocephala
children.description: A seedeater with a black head, rufous breast, grey back, and white belly. It is found in grasslands of southern South America.

children.name: Sporophila ruficollis
children.description: A seedeater with a black throat, rufous collar, grey back, and white underparts. It is found in grasslands of central South America.

children.name: Sporophila pilzei
children.description: A seedeater with a black head and breast, grey back, and white belly. It is endemic to the Cerrado region of Brazil.

children.name: Sporophila telasco
children.description: A seedeater with a chestnut throat, grey back, and white underparts. It is found in dry valleys of the Andes.
